column4:
- I have an autistic 11-year-old struggling with emotional regulation, focus, and negative self-talk.
- Therapy helps her feel happier and learn coping mechanisms; she prefers to keep session details private.
- We sought therapy after school issues and suicidal statements; immediate access was difficult due to waitlists.
- We reached help by calling emergency services, which connected us to a local provider.
- I don’t trust AI in mental health; I worry about data breaches and AI missing distress cues; small groups and video could help if human-led.
column5: Protective parent insists on human-led care
column7: I have children between 6 and 12 years old
column8: Sports or physical activities, Art or music classes, Therapy or counseling
column9: Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem, Emotional regulation challenges, Behavioral issues at school
column10: It was good and something she really needed. My 11-year-old, who is autistic, has trouble with emotional regulation, staying calm, and maintaining focus, and therapy helps her feel better about herself and counter negative self-talk. After sessions she’s usually happier and more active; she keeps some details private but says she’s learning coping mechanisms. The last session helped her maintain control and feel more confident.
column11: She was having a lot of issues at school, lashing out and attacking other students. And also, unfortunately, she had mentioned that she does not want to be alive anymore.
column12: Getting her immediate help was the hardest part because most places had long wait times. After she said she didn’t want to live anymore—even if she didn’t fully understand it—we needed urgent support and that was hard to find. We called emergency services, who connected us to a local company.
column13: She’s only done individual therapy because we felt group therapy wouldn’t help her as much and preferred one-on-one. I worry a group wouldn’t give her enough attention and she might feel embarrassed and hold back. If everyone in the group, not just the counselor, offered reassurance, that could help her open up since she’s honest but very shy.
column14: I think it would need to be a small group, nothing with a lot of people, and one that specifically focused on the issues of self-confidence, self-doubts, negative self-talk, feelings of suicide, just in a smaller group so everyone in there can get the attention they need.
column15: Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Somewhat important | Consistent session structure: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Very important | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Affordable cost: Somewhat important
column16: I don’t like it. Using AI in mental health loses human touch, and AI often gets things wrong. I worry it would misrepresent things, mishandle scheduling, and fail to account for each individual child. I use AI as a tool at work, but not for medical or mental health.
column18: I would need anything AI in that kind of context strictly only used for scheduling, and then any of that scheduling has to be confirmed by a real person, not just relied solely on the AI. So it's used as a tool and not as a crutch.
column19: I worry about data being stored with AI and potential breaches. I also fear AI won’t recognize when my child is shutting down or distressed; I need a human for that and don’t want AI involved in those moments.
column20: I’m fine with video group therapy as long as it’s a small group. It might make it easier for her because she can ignore other kids if she needs to.
column22: I don't really think anything. I just don't trust AI in any medical or health-related field.
column24: I gave my child the option to have me present in individual sessions or to meet alone with the therapist.
column30:
• Which of the following best describes your household? → I have children between 6 and 12 years old
• Which of these activities has your child participated in during the past 12 months? → Sports or physical activities, Art or music classes, Therapy or counseling
• Which of the following challenges has your child experienced? Select all that apply. → Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem, Emotional regulation challenges, Behavioral issues at school
• Tell me about the last time your child attended a therapy or counseling session. What was it like?
 → It was good. It was something that they really needed to have done. My oldest, particularly 11 years old, has been having a lot of trouble with emotional regulations, a lot of trouble keeping calm, maintaining focus. She's autistic, so it really helps. And it helps her feel good about herself because she has a lot of negative self-thoughts. And her typical therapy session, like the last one, really helped her try to maintain control.
• The negative self-thoughts you mentioned — when she leaves a session feeling better about herself, what does that look like at home? Do you notice a difference in her?
 → Usually it means that she is much happier, active. She doesn't— she likes to not tell us everything that goes on in therapy so she can keep that to herself, but she does tell us that she feels happy that she's learning new coping mechanisms.
• It sounds like those coping mechanisms are giving her something real to hold onto. What made you decide to seek therapy or support for her in the first place?
 → She was having a lot of issues at school, lashing out and attacking other students. And also, unfortunately, she had mentioned that she does not want to be alive anymore.
• That must have been a terrifying moment to hear from your child. What has been the most frustrating part of finding and maintaining the right therapy for her since then?
 → And really the hardest part was getting her immediate help. Most places had long wait times. Uh, hearing that she didn't want to live anymore, I know she didn't fully understand what it meant, but we needed immediate help and that was hard to find.
• That gap between urgency and availability is one of the hardest things a parent can face. When you finally did find help, was it through a specific type of program, or did you have to piece things together?

 → We had called emergency services when she mentioned that she wanted to die. We knew that she didn't know exactly what that meant, but we knew immediate action had to be taken, so we called an emergency service who put us in touch with a local company.
• It took a crisis to unlock the door — that's such a broken part of the system. Has your daughter ever participated in group therapy, or has it been individual sessions?
 → It's always been individual. We didn't feel that a group therapy would help her. We wanted more one-on-one. We did give also her the option whether she wanted us there with her or if she wanted to do it by herself with her therapist.
• Given that you went with individual sessions, I'm curious — if you imagine your daughter in a group therapy setting, what concerns would come up for you? Especially thinking about whether a quieter or more overwhelmed child would actually get the attention they need.
 → I do worry that they would get not enough attention that they need to help their issues. I do also get nervous that she would feel embarrassed and not really tell everything that was going on.
• The embarrassment piece is interesting — feeling like she has to hold back in front of peers. What would need to be different about a group setting for you to feel like she'd actually open up and get what she needs?

 → I think as long as she was getting a lot of assurance from everyone in the group, not just from the counselor, I think that would go a long way to have her really open up about her anxieties and fears in a group setting. She's a very honest person but very shy.
• That combination — honest but shy — really paints a picture of her. What would an ideal group therapy session look like for your child?

 → I think it would need to be a small group, nothing with a lot of people, and one that specifically focused on the issues of self-confidence, self-doubts, negative self-talk, feelings of suicide, just in a smaller group so everyone in there can get the attention they need.
• Small group, focused on the exact things she's struggling with — that makes a lot of sense for her. How important are each of the following in a group therapy program for your child?

 → Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Consistent session structure: Critical | Affordable cost: Somewhat important | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Somewhat important
• I'd like to share an idea with you. Imagine a group therapy program for children where a licensed therapist leads every session, but is supported by an AI assistant. The AI helps manage the session — for example, making sure every child gets a turn to speak, guiding structured activities like storytelling or role-playing, and gently encouraging quieter children to participate. The therapist remains fully in control and can step in at any time. The AI simply helps the session run more smoothly and inclusively. → Continue
• What's your initial reaction to that idea?
 → I don't really like that idea. I think anything that is using AI, particularly for mental health needs, is losing some of that human touch. And AI can only do so much. It's only being told— it can only do what it's been told or what it's learned. And there are so many times I see AI get things wrong.
• The "getting things wrong" part — can you say more about what that would look like in this context? What's the kind of mistake you'd be most worried about an AI making in a room full of vulnerable kids?

 → I'm afraid that it would misrepresent things, not schedule things enough, really not take into account each individual child, even if it's been told to or programmed to. It just, I'm not confident in AI enough. And it is something that I use as a tool at work, but for medical and mental health reasons, I don't think it's a great idea.
• You draw a clear line between AI as a work tool and AI in something as sensitive as your daughter's mental health — that distinction makes a lot of sense given what's at stake for her. How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?

 → Very uncomfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → I would need anything AI in that kind of context strictly only used for scheduling, and then any of that scheduling has to be confirmed by a real person, not just relied solely on the AI. So it's used as a tool and not as a crutch.
• So AI strictly in the background, with a human always verifying — keeping it out of the actual therapeutic conversation entirely. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI being involved in your child's therapy?

 → I would worry about information stored with the AI and data breaches.
• Privacy is a real concern, especially with something as sensitive as mental health records. Beyond data security, do you worry at all about the AI missing emotional cues in the moment — like not recognizing when a child is shutting down or getting distressed?

 → I would. AI is not trained well enough to recognize those things. I need an actual human there to recognize when my child is shutting down or distressed. I don't want AI involved with that at all.
• Given everything you've shared about your daughter — her shyness, the emotional intensity of what she's working through — how would you feel about her attending group therapy via video call rather than in person?

 → I mean, I think that's fine as long as it's a small group, like I said.
• Does the video format feel like it would make it easier or harder for her to open up, compared to being in a room with other kids?
 → I think that might make it easier for her because she can also ignore the other kids in the room if she needs to.
• That's an interesting angle — the screen as a kind of buffer. If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?

 → Definitely would not try it
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?
 → I don't really think anything. I just don't trust AI in any medical or health-related field.

column4:
- My child (6–12) has faced bullying, social anxiety, low confidence, and emotional regulation challenges.
- Therapy has significantly improved her emotional regulation and ability to separate situations from self.
- We’ve had to switch therapists multiple times; losing a favored therapist made her very sad.
- Open to group therapy if it’s small, well-mediated, safe, and peer-connected; virtual format feels familiar (Discord).
- Comfortable with AI for logistics only; wants strong privacy, therapist control, and sensitivity to nonverbal cues.
column5: Caring parent seeking safe, peer-connected support
column7: I have children between 6 and 12 years old
column8: Religious or community programs, Art or music classes, Sports or physical activities, Therapy or counseling
column9: Difficulty making friends or socializing, Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem, Emotional regulation challenges
column10: I sat in on her last therapy session. We talked about what’s been happening at school, especially the bullying, and reflected on how far she’s come this year with emotional regulation. Therapy has been super helpful—she’s much better at separating the situation from herself and choosing how to respond, like calmly and firmly talking through a recent friend conflict instead of getting angry.
column11: It was definitely bullying, and one of the things I asked her therapist was— because her therapist asked me, um, what— in what ways do you want her to, like, have help? And I said definitely her self-confidence and, you know, knowing how special and smart and wonderful she is, because the bullying was really wearing her down.
column12: The initial match was frustrating—we did a full session with a therapist we thought was right, then got redirected to another therapist. My daughter really connected with the second therapist, but she’s leaving for school, so now we have to switch again. This will be the third therapist in less than a year, and my daughter is sad and sullen about losing that connection after her last session and hug.
column13: She hasn’t been in group therapy. I think she’d struggle to speak up at first, especially in big groups of strangers, but in a smaller, more intimate group she’d likely open up and thrive. As long as someone mediates to ensure everyone gets a turn, I don’t think participation would be an issue.
column14: An ideal session would connect her with peers her age who share similar issues—bullying, social anxiety, and confidence worries—so she realizes she’s not alone. A safe, judgment-free space outside of school where she can be herself and connect with other kids would be key.
column15: Consistent session structure: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Somewhat important | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Critical | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Critical | Affordable cost: Very important
column16: It seems interesting—an AI could help keep sessions structured, timed, and ensure everyone gets a turn. I still feel a real person is warmer and easier to talk to. With AI handling logistics, the therapist could focus fully on the kids, which sounds great.
column18: To be, fully comfortable, I think just like communication with the therapist through email. That's kind of what I have now with her therapist. Communication, updates, things like that. Getting to know the therapist and the structure. And other than that, like, I can't think of much else.
column19: I’m fine with AI handling timers and logistics. I’d be worried if AI gave therapy advice since it isn’t always correct. If the therapist used a locked-down, kid-appropriate, privacy-safe AI, I’d be comfortable. I also worry AI might miss nonverbal cues like facial expressions and body language that signal emotions.
column20: I’m fine with virtual therapy. It might be easier to get to know people from her bedroom, and she’s used to Discord calls, so it could feel familiar.
column22: If I were to choose this over her current therapy arrangement, it would be for interacting with the other kids. And like I said before, just realizing and talking to other kids who are going through the same stuff that she is, because sometimes I think she feels kind of alone in it. Because like, how do you find, you know, other kids that are going through this and like being bullied and have that kind of like confidence issue? I think that would be the thing that would make me choose the group therapy.
column23: Parent of child 6–12, Child in therapy
column30:
• Which of the following best describes your household? → I have children between 6 and 12 years old
• Which of these activities has your child participated in during the past 12 months? → Religious or community programs, Art or music classes, Sports or physical activities, Therapy or counseling
• Which of the following challenges has your child experienced? Select all that apply. → Difficulty making friends or socializing, Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem, Emotional regulation challenges
• Tell me about the last time your child attended a therapy or counseling session. What was it like?
 → So I actually was, I sat in on her last therapy session and we talked about just things that have been going on in school. She's gone through a lot of like bullying and we talked about how far she's come this year and how much better she is at regulating her emotions. And she really has come a very, very long way and therapy has been super helpful in helping her achieve that.
• That's wonderful to hear she's made so much progress with emotional regulation. What specific changes have you noticed in how she handles difficult situations now compared to before therapy?
 → So she doesn't get, like, angry, upset anymore. Like, the other day, for example, she had a falling out with some of her friends, and I could hear her sternly talking to them. And so I knocked on her door, and she was like, hey, what's up? And I'm like, are you okay? And she's like, yeah, I'm fine, I'm just upset at the situation. Things like that, you know, where she's able to separate the situation from, like, herself and decide how she's gonna feel about it.
• What made you decide to seek therapy or support for your child in the first place?
 → It was definitely bullying, and one of the things I asked her therapist was— because her therapist asked me, um, what— in what ways do you want her to, like, have help? And I said definitely her self-confidence and, you know, knowing how special and smart and wonderful she is, because the bullying was really wearing her down.
• What has been the most frustrating part of finding and maintaining the right therapy for your child?

 → So, the most frustrating part was the first, like, initial meeting because we sat down with who we thought was, like, the right therapist and we had this full session. We got to know her and then she emailed me one day and she was like, "I feel like this therapist would be a better fit," and she was, but now that therapist, the one that Erin really likes, my daughter, was like, you know, I'm studying in school and I'm leaving soon. And so now we have to switch therapists again. So this will be the third therapist we've been through in like less than a year.
• How has your daughter handled these transitions between therapists, especially after building that connection with the second one?

 → She's very sullen about it. Um, we're actually, like, between— right now, the therapist she really likes, she just had her last session with her, and she gave her a big hug, and she just seems really sad about it because they do have a connection, and she looked forward to seeing her every week, but there's not really anything we can do about it.
• Has your child been in group therapy? If so, think back to a specific session - how engaged was she, and what did she tell you about it afterwards?

 → She has not been in group therapy, no.
• What do you imagine group therapy would be like for your daughter? Do you have any concerns about it, especially given that she's been working on social anxiety and confidence?

 → I think initially she would probably have a hard time fitting in, speaking up rather. But I think once she got to know some of the people in the group therapy, I think she would really open up because that's just how she is. Initially, she hates like big groups of like strangers, but if it were like a more intimate setting with like not too many people, I think she would really thrive.
• In a group setting with other kids, do you worry that quieter or shyer children like your daughter might not get enough attention or opportunities to participate?

 → I think that so long as there is someone there to, uh, kind of mediate and make sure that everyone has a chance to speak, I don't think it would be an issue
• What would an ideal group therapy session look like for your daughter?

 → an ideal group therapy session would be a chance for her to maybe, like, talk to other kids who have the same sort of, like, issues that she does and worries and that social anxiety. Talking to kids her own age and realizing, hey, these kids, you know, are going through the same thing that I am, I think that would be really eye-opening for her.
• Beyond connecting with peers who share similar experiences, what else would make a group session truly effective for her?

 → I think in general, just being able to connect with other kids without school, without the bullying, just like having kind of an open canvas of, you know, in just a safe setting where she can be herself and not worry about being judged.
• How important are each of the following in a group therapy program for your child?
 → Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Critical | Affordable cost: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Somewhat important | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Critical | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Consistent session structure: Very important
• I'd like to share an idea with you. Imagine a group therapy program for children where a licensed therapist leads every session, but is supported by an AI assistant. The AI helps manage the session — for example, making sure every child gets a turn to speak, guiding structured activities like storytelling or role-playing, and gently encouraging quieter children to participate. The therapist remains fully in control and can step in at any time. The AI simply helps the session run more smoothly and inclusively. → Continue
• What's your initial reaction to that idea?

 → That could be an interesting idea because I think in terms of like organized thoughts, having like an AI guide would be really good to keep like the group therapy sessions structured and it would be able to be like properly timed between, you know, letting everyone speak and activities and things like that.
• You mentioned the structure and timing benefits. Are there any concerns or hesitations you have about AI being involved in this way?

 → I just feel like an actual person is more warm and inviting, and I think, like, easier to talk to in most instances.
• I hear that concern about warmth. To clarify - in this model, the licensed therapist is still the main person leading and connecting with the kids. The AI would just handle logistics like timers and turn-taking. Does that distinction change how you feel about it?

 → Yeah, I mean, that sounds like a great setup because then the therapist can focus on, you know, the kids, just the kids. She doesn't have to worry—or he or she doesn't have to worry about timing or like, you know, just the little things. That, I think that would be a really good way to give the therapist the maximum amount of attention and time to the kids.
• How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?

 → Very comfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → To be, fully comfortable, I think just like communication with the therapist through email. That's kind of what I have now with her therapist. Communication, updates, things like that. Getting to know the therapist and the structure. And other than that, like, I can't think of much else.
• What concerns, if any, do you have about AI being involved in your child's therapy?

 → I don't have many concerns at all if it's just like timers and like logistical things. If it were AI giving therapy advice, I would be a little worried because I know that AI isn't always 100% correct.
• That makes sense. What about privacy and safety - do you have any concerns about how the AI might handle sensitive information your child shares, or whether it could say something inappropriate?

 → I think if the therapist locked down a specific safe AI that they knew wouldn't share, like, information and was appropriate for kids, I wouldn't have any issues with it
• Do you worry at all that the AI might miss important emotional cues that a human therapist would catch - like if a child seems upset but isn't speaking up?

 → Actually, yeah, like, like facial expressions or body language. That's a really good point. Little things like that, because there are things outside of spoken word that, you know, indicate emotions.
• How would you feel about your child attending group therapy via video call rather than in person?


 → yeah, I'm fine with, you know, virtual therapy and things like that
• What appeals to you about the virtual format, especially for group therapy?

 → I feel like it'd be a lot easier to get to know all of those people from the comfort of your own bedroom. She's used to like Discord calls and things like that too, so it might seem a bit more of a familiar environment.
• If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?

 → Probably would try it
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?


 → If I were to choose this over her current therapy arrangement, it would be for interacting with the other kids. And like I said before, just realizing and talking to other kids who are going through the same stuff that she is, because sometimes I think she feels kind of alone in it. Because like, how do you find, you know, other kids that are going through this and like being bullied and have that kind of like confidence issue? I think that would be the thing that would make me choose the group therapy.

• What do you imagine group therapy would be like for your child? Do you have any concerns about whether a quieter or shyer child like yours would get enough attention and participation?

 → I think it would take her a while to warm up to it
• What specifically concerns you about that warm-up period? Do you worry she might not participate enough or get overlooked?

 → Getting overlooked
• What would an ideal group therapy session look like for your child?

 → A very small group and a very understanding therapist.
• What does "very small" mean to you - how many children would be ideal?

 → 5 or less
• How important are each of the following in a group therapy program for your child?
 → Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Critical | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Somewhat important | Affordable cost: Somewhat important | Consistent session structure: Critical
• I'd like to share an idea with you. Imagine a group therapy program for children where a licensed therapist leads every session, but is supported by an AI assistant. The AI helps manage the session — for example, making sure every child gets a turn to speak, guiding structured activities like storytelling or role-playing, and gently encouraging quieter children to participate. The therapist remains fully in control and can step in at any time. The AI simply helps the session run more smoothly and inclusively. → Continue
• What's your initial reaction to that idea?

 → It seems like a great idea
• What specifically excites you about it? And do you have any concerns or reservations?

 → No I have no concerns and I like everything about it.
• That's wonderful to hear! What aspect appeals to you most - is it the idea that every child gets a turn, the structure the AI provides, or something else?


 → Definitely every child getting a turn
• How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?

 → Somewhat comfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → That the claims are all true
• What specifically would you need to see or hear to believe those claims are true?

 → See a session in progress
• What concerns, if any, do you have about AI being involved in your child's therapy?


 → None at all
• Even things like privacy, or whether the AI might miss an important emotional cue from your child? No worries at all about those aspects?

 → None
• How would you feel about your child attending group therapy via video call rather than in person?


 → I would be fine with that
• Would telehealth make it easier or harder for your child to participate compared to in-person sessions?

 → Probably a little easier
• If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?

 → Probably would try it
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?

column4:
- I have a child aged 6–12 with focus challenges, social anxiety, and some academic struggles.
- We’ve been in therapy about 3 months, with family check-ins and private one-on-one time my child enjoys.
- Finding covered care for her age was a year-long struggle; insurance pushed telehealth that didn’t cover her age, damaging my trust.
- In groups she might zone out unless engaged; small, active (art) sessions with deliberate inclusion would suit her.
- I’m only comfortable with AI in a narrow, background role; prefer transcripts, strict privacy, and in-person sessions over telehealth with AI.
column5: Cautious parent prioritizing human-led, active therapy
column7: I have children between 6 and 12 years old
column8: Therapy or counseling, Art or music classes, Sports or physical activities
column9: Academic struggles, Difficulty with focus or attention, Shyness or social anxiety
column10: Therapy has been really great. Sessions are 45 minutes: about 15–25 minutes as a family discussing concerns, progress, and goals with therapist feedback and suggestions, then we wrap up and my child has private one-on-one time with the therapist. She understands that necessary information will be shared with us, but it’s her chance to talk privately with a trusted adult. She seems to really enjoy that one-on-one time and, over the ~3 months we’ve been attending, it’s helped her build rapport with the therapist.
column11: I think it was sort of a mixture of things. She's at an age where we were starting to notice an increase in social anxiety, maybe a little bit of a decrease in confidence. And we were also having some academic struggles and, you know, she's very bright, but we've always been a little concerned about her lack of focus. We weren't sure if there was anything underlying there, or if it was just developmental. And we, you know, decided to watch it, and if we saw it start to interfere academically, we'd step in and sort of see what was going on. And then that ended up being the case this year. I think as she ages and there's less one-on-one attention from a teacher, her... You know, they say her behavior's great, but her ability to focus has really been a challenge, and it did start impacting her grades.
column12: The initial process was awful. Finding someone who sees children her age and is covered by our insurance was a nightmare; we tried for almost a year. Insurance kept steering us to telehealth because it was cheap or free, but they don’t cover under a certain age, so that didn’t work. There were no local providers for her age group with openings. It damaged my trust in the insurance and was frustrating that they pushed an option they should know wasn’t available for her age.
column13: She hasn’t done group therapy with other children, only brief family segments at the start. Her engagement varies; because of focus challenges she zones out unless directly addressed, though she’s generally positive. She says if she isn’t required to think and respond actively, it’s hard to focus. In a kids’ group, she’d likely be quiet, well-behaved, zone out, and get overshadowed by more outgoing kids, but not see it as negative—just not very beneficial. As a former teacher, I’d want the facilitator to track participation and intentionally pull in quieter kids.
column14: Ideal group: small, peers close in age, and active—especially art therapy—since she does well when actively doing things and struggles with sit-and-listen formats. Signs it’s working: she engages with peers and not just the adult or activity, shows enthusiasm, asks questions, and expresses her needs appropriately. I’d also want her to talk about it afterward, since sharing can be hard for her.
column15: Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Very important | Consistent session structure: Somewhat important | Affordable cost: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Very important | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Very important
column16: Comfortable with AI for background tracking—who has spoken, engagement—and making suggestions to the therapist. Not comfortable with kids interacting with the AI directly. Kids may not understand what AI is, and shy kids like mine could depend on it instead of engaging with people, which would be a step backward.
column18: I’d need strong safety and oversight, including a transcript parents can access shortly after each session (end of session is fine). I want visibility into exactly how the AI was used and how she interacted with it, so I can gauge changes given I know her best initially.
column19: I’m okay with AI giving neutral prompts like reminders to speak, but I don’t want it engaging in social give-and-take or my child responding to the AI—responses should be to the group/therapist. The therapist, not AI, should handle emotional cues. With tightly limited programming on what the AI can address, I’m less worried about inappropriate content. For privacy, transcripts in a group would need parent consent and de-identified names; no use for studies or training; storage should follow medical data laws.
column20: Much less comfortable with video sessions if AI is involved. I want a tangible person in the room to keep clear boundaries between AI and human; with both on a screen, younger kids may blur that line.
column22: I wouldn’t choose it because my child’s current copay is lower; given higher cost and my concerns, it doesn’t make sense. Even at equal cost, the AI component would still be a dealbreaker unless its scope were limited much more.
column30:
• Which of the following best describes your household? → I have children between 6 and 12 years old
• Which of these activities has your child participated in during the past 12 months? → Therapy or counseling, Art or music classes, Sports or physical activities
• Which of the following challenges has your child experienced? Select all that apply. → Academic struggles, Difficulty with focus or attention, Shyness or social anxiety
• Tell me about the last time your child attended a therapy or counseling session. What was it like?
 → Okay, can you hear me? So, therapy has been really great. Her sessions all kind of take the same sort of framework. So, we spend the first, like, the sessions are 45 minutes long. We spend the first 15 to 20, sometimes 25 minutes, sort of discussing as a group, as a family, what concerns we have, the progress we're seeing, the things that we think we really need to work on, and then the therapist kind of gives us feedback, makes suggestions, we discuss it with my child, and see, you know, what they think of the suggestions and things that are given, and then we sort of wrap up with the whole group and then my child gets a little alone time with a therapist to discuss private matters. You know, she has an understanding that anything that is necessary to tell the parents will get reported back, but for the most part, it's a chance for her to have some one-on-one with a trusted adult to say things that maybe she's not comfortable saying with me and, you know, that maybe I don't 100% need to know.
• That private time sounds especially meaningful. How has your child seemed to feel about having that one-on-one space with the therapist?

 → Um, I think it might be like her favorite component to things, um, especially since this has only been going on for, oh, I want to say 3 or so months. Um, so those sessions have been really important with her being able to sort of build a rapport with the therapist, and she seems to really enjoy it.
• What made you decide to seek therapy or support for your child in the first place?
 → I think it was sort of a mixture of things. She's at an age where we were starting to notice an increase in social anxiety, maybe a little bit of a decrease in confidence. And we were also having some academic struggles and, you know, she's very bright, but we've always been a little concerned about her lack of focus. We weren't sure if there was anything underlying there, or if it was just developmental. And we, you know, decided to watch it, and if we saw it start to interfere academically, we'd step in and sort of see what was going on. And then that ended up being the case this year. I think as she ages and there's less one-on-one attention from a teacher, her... You know, they say her behavior's great, but her ability to focus has really been a challenge, and it did start impacting her grades.
• What has been the most frustrating part of finding and maintaining the right therapy for your child?

 → Oh, the initial process of getting into therapy for a child her age was awful. Just finding someone who would serve her that was also covered by our insurance was a nightmare. We'd been trying for, goodness, almost a year, really. It got to the point where she's kind of aged into a group where she was allowed to be in and get covered because our insurance kept telling us to go with telehealth because it was really cheap or free, but they don't cover under a certain age. And so that didn't work. And there was no one in the area that covered her age group that had openings.
• That sounds exhausting. When insurance kept steering you toward telehealth that wouldn’t actually cover her age, what did that do to your trust in the whole process?

 → I was— I mean, it certainly damaged my trust in the insurance. Maybe that's always been a little bit rough because we all know that insurance is incredibly expensive and it does not always, uh, help in the ways it seems like it ought to. Um, but it was particularly frustrating that they were— that, that was so insistent, like that's the direction everybody needed to go in. And you would think that they would know that under a certain age you can't use that service. And it was very frustrating, um, and, and very worrying that they just weren't aware of that.
• If your child has been in group therapy, think back to a specific session. How engaged was your child? What did they tell you about it afterwards?

 → Um, she hasn't done group therapy in terms of like her and other children. Um, the closest we've gotten has been the little family sessions at the beginning, and her engagement varies. Um, I definitely see— again, we're going for lack of focus, so she'll zone out unless like specifically addressed. Um, for the most part, she seems very positive about it. She just says that, you know, if she's not actively having to think to respond, it's hard for her to focus.
• That makes sense. If she were in a group with other kids, what do you imagine it would be like for her—especially as someone who can zone out unless she’s directly brought in?

 → I imagine it would be kind of how she reports things happening at school. She's very quiet, she's very well behaved, and she can zone out. And she's very bright, so for the most part she can kind of scramble to get back in without it being necessarily detected that much. So I think that that's what would end up happening, is she would she would definitely lack focus and get sort of overshadowed by more outgoing people.
• You know her patterns really well. What would need to happen in that kind of group so quieter kids like her actually get enough attention and participate, instead of getting overshadowed?

 → So I used to be a teacher, so I know the strategy that I used in a group setting like that is I would have like a list of the names and I would just put sort of a tick next to each person as they engaged. And if I saw somebody who didn't have ticks and like there were other people who had like 2 or more, I would intentionally sort of pull that person in, especially if I knew like something they were interested in or a thing that I could sort of guide the conversation towards.
• That’s a very concrete approach. If a group leader didn’t notice who was getting overshadowed the way you would, what do you think your daughter would experience in that session?

 → Um, for her, I don't think it would be necessarily a negative experience. I think for some kids they would certainly see it as them not mattering, but the way her mind works, I think she would just get distracted and zone out, and I don't think it would be negative for her, but I don't think she would benefit at all either.
• What would an ideal group therapy session look like for your child?

 → I think an ideal session would be definitely a smaller group, kids close in age to her. Ideally, like, an art therapy, I think, would work really well, something active, because, you know, she doesn't have a hard time in the classes where she's actively doing things. It's where she has to sort of sit and get information and then occasionally respond, and that's where she really struggles.
• Something active seems key for her. In that smaller, art-based group, what would you hope she’d be doing or saying by the end that would tell you it was really working?

 → Um, I think I would see a difference in a couple of things. If I, uh, if I noticed that she was really engaging, not just with the, the art aspect or the adult, but the other kids around her and sort of a give and take, um, and, and seemed very enthusiastic and interested. Also, if I saw her like really asking questions and like expressing her own needs too, um, in a way that was really healthy and age appropriate, of course, uh, that would be key. And I think also if she, if she would report afterwards, if she would talk about it, because getting her to talk about things can be so difficult sometimes.
• How important are each of the following in a group therapy program for your child?
 → Consistent session structure: Somewhat important | Affordable cost: Very important | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Very important | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Very important | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical
• I'd like to share an idea with you. Imagine a group therapy program for children where a licensed therapist leads every session, but is supported by an AI assistant. The AI helps manage the session — for example, making sure every child gets a turn to speak, guiding structured activities like storytelling or role-playing, and gently encouraging quieter children to participate. The therapist remains fully in control and can step in at any time. The AI simply helps the session run more smoothly and inclusively. → Continue
• What's your initial reaction to that idea?
 → I'm pretty comfortable with the idea of AI doing tracking components, like, you know, tracking who has spoken and been involved, and maybe making suggestions specifically to the therapist. I'm less comfortable with the idea of the kids interacting with AI in any way.
• That distinction is really clear. What feels okay about the AI staying in the background for tracking, and what gives you pause about your child interacting with it directly?

 → I think for kids, that understanding of what an AI is and its capabilities is a lot more vague than an adult who would have a firm understanding of what they were getting involved with.
• That uncertainty seems important. What worries you most could happen if a child her age doesn’t really understand what the AI is or what it can do?


 → I think for her, especially kids that are really shy, it could be very easy to sort of depend on that interaction, because it's easier than having to interact with people, which is concerning. That would certainly be a step backwards in that aspect.
• How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?
 → Somewhat comfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → I think that maybe I could feel comfortable if there was like a transcript that parents could access so they could see, you know, exactly how the AI was utilized, exactly how she interacted with it fairly immediately. And then, you know, at the end of every session, because— or even real time, but I think maybe at the end of the session is more feasible.
• Having that visibility sounds important. Would the main reason for wanting a transcript be safety and oversight, or is there something else you’d want to be able to check for?

 → I think it would largely be safety and oversight. I mean, again, if there was a transcript, that could be another thing the AI is doing, I suppose. But if there was a transcript, it would be nice to be able to have a clearer understanding of how she's interacting with things, just because the therapist is getting to know her very well, but at least initially, I mean, I know her better, so I could gauge how different things really are.
• What concerns, if any, do you have about AI being involved in your child's therapy?

 → Um, I think primarily, like, I wouldn't want the AI to do any sort of... Like, it could just be like, "Hey, Corey, you haven't talked in a while." Um, or, "Hey, Chris, you need to go..." I'm sorry. Or, "Hey, Chris, you need to do XYZ." Like, I would be okay with the prompting aspect, but I would be less comfortable with, um, any kind of social, like, give and take. I, I wouldn't want that. So it would have to be like she's not responding to the AI. They're only responding to the, uh, group.
• You’re drawing a pretty firm line around the AI not becoming a social partner. Beyond that, do you worry it might miss important emotional cues or say something inappropriate in a sensitive moment?

 → I don't think it should be the job of the AI to find emotional cues. That seems like it would be way more important for the therapist. In terms of inappropriate, I think with a very limited programming to make sure that only certain things are allowed to be addressed, I'm a little less concerned about that.
• Got it — for you, the bigger issue is keeping the AI narrowly limited rather than trusting it to read emotions. What kind of privacy protections or boundaries around recording, storage, or data use would you want in place?

 → I think that, you know, in a group setting it would be very, very hard to have the transcriptions or things like that only available to each kid's parent for what they say because the give and take in the group is kind of important. I think if the parents involved signed off, I'd be okay with a transcript being given that had dummy names put in place, like no identifiable about that specific kid. It would have to be sort of programmed to plug in that other name. I definitely wouldn't want it to be used to, in like for studies or changing the AI or anything like that. I think that needs to be more controlled things. In terms of storage, I mean, I guess they would have to follow the legal storing for all medical data.
• That makes sense. How would you feel about your child attending group therapy via video call rather than in person?

 → I'm much less comfortable with it via video call if it's going to incorporate AI. I think that it would be important for there to be a tangible person in the room to mark that difference between the AI and the person.
• That difference seems especially important to you. What is it about video plus AI together that feels less comfortable than having a tangible person there in the room?

 → I just think, with younger kids, it would be easier for those boundaries to blur as to exactly what the AI is, being a computer program, if the AI and the therapist are both on the screen
• If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?
 → Definitely would not try it
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?

 → Honestly, my child's copay for her therapy sessions is much lower than that in terms of cost, so the potential concerns and it being more expensive, it just wouldn't make sense.
• That makes sense. If cost were equal to what you pay now, is there anything else that could realistically make this worth considering, or would the AI piece still be a dealbreaker?

 → I think the AI piece would still be a deal breaker, um, in that terms. Um, I'm trying to think of when I would be okay with it. I think if they limited the AI's scope a lot more, um, then I might consider it.

column4:
- Sought therapy to help her child attend school and function.
- Tried about three clinics before finding a fit.
- Child struggles with anxiety, depression, shyness, and social anxiety; group settings overwhelm her.
- Prefers very small group therapy and options to engage without being forced.
- Open to AI-supported therapy if human-guided, with options like texting or AI speaking for her child.
column5: Parent Seeking Gentle, Human-Guided AI Support
column7: I have children between 6 and 12 years old
column8: Art or music classes, Therapy or counseling, Sports or physical activities, Religious or community programs
column9: Academic struggles, Emotional regulation challenges, Difficulty with focus or attention, Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem, Difficulty making friends or socializing
column10: My child attends therapy for anxiety and depression. Sessions last 30–60 minutes and cover what's bothering her and medication changes. She usually enjoys going, but after sessions she can be either okay or very triggered and emotionally upset as difficult emotions are processed.
column11: I think it was needed for her to be able to maintain a good ability to attend school and function.
column12: It's been hard to find a therapist my child enjoys and will open up to. We went through at least three clinics, about three doctors.
column13: She did group therapy before and did not like it. There were too many people, she felt uncomfortable speaking, got lost in the group, stared off, and froze even when encouraged.
column14: An ideal group would let her interact freely without being forced. A much smaller group size—one to two people instead of five to ten—would suit her better.
column15: Consistent session structure: Somewhat important | Affordable cost: Very important | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Very important
column16: I think that is a great idea. I wish each child had access to their own personal AI so they can speak openly and fairly, which could prevent freezing up and let AI speak for her when she can't.
column18: I think each child should have access to their own ability to opt out or ability to use AI if they are comfortable and like what it is capable of.
column19: AI is positive if it's positive and controlled. It should be guided by a human and not be fully AI, with a person able to step in at all times. I have some privacy concerns but I'm more worried about AI taking therapy in the wrong direction.
column20: I'm unsure she'd enjoy video calls; using the phone itself can cause anxiety. She might warm up over time, especially if she knows it's a guided computer rather than a real person.
column22: I think this would be a very convenient option to be able to integrate her ability to speak through the AI or communicate with it in more than one way, especially outside of just talking. Having an AI she could text to or select options as to how she feels, or an AI that could communicate to the therapist for her what she has said
column23: Parent of child 6–12, Child in therapy and group sessions
column30:
• Which of the following best describes your household? → I have children between 6 and 12 years old
• Which of these activities has your child participated in during the past 12 months? → Art or music classes, Therapy or counseling, Sports or physical activities, Religious or community programs
• Which of the following challenges has your child experienced? Select all that apply. → Academic struggles, Emotional regulation challenges, Difficulty with focus or attention, Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem, Difficulty making friends or socializing
• Tell me about the last time your child attended a therapy or counseling session. What was it like?
 → My child attends therapy sessions to address anxiety and depression. She typically goes and enjoys her visits. It lasts about 30 minutes to an hour. They attend the counseling sessions and discuss the things that are bothering them and medication changes.
• It's nice to hear she actually enjoys going — that's not always the case. You mentioned they discuss things that are bothering her and medication changes; how does she seem after sessions, emotionally?
 → Sometimes she is good, other times she is very triggered and emotionally upset, which is to be expected when you run through difficult emotions.
• That makes a lot of sense — processing hard things stirs them up before it settles them down. What made you decide to seek therapy or support for your child in the first place?
 → I think it was needed for her to be able to maintain a good ability to attend school and function.
• What has been the most frustrating part of finding and maintaining the right therapy for your child?
 → It has been hard to find a therapist that my child enjoys going to and that does not mind opening up to.
• Finding that right fit — someone she actually wants to open up to — can take a lot of trial and error. How many therapists did you go through before finding one that clicked?
 → I think we ended up going through at least 3 clinics, so about 3 doctors.
• Three clinics before finding the right one — that's a real investment of time and emotional energy for both of you. Has your child ever participated in group therapy, or has it always been one-on-one sessions?
 → She has participated in group therapy before and did not like it. There were too many people and she did not feel comfortable opening up or even speaking.
• That's really telling — she already struggles with shyness and social anxiety, and being in a room with too many people made it harder to participate, not easier. Did she ever feel like the therapist noticed she wasn't engaging, or did she kind of just get lost in the group?

 → The therapist noticed that she wasn't able to engage and that she got lost in the group and just stared off.
• That image of her just staring off while the session moved on around her is really striking. When the therapist noticed she wasn't engaging, did they do anything to try to bring her in, or did the session just continue without her?
 → They spoke to her and tried to encourage her, and instead of speaking back, she sat quietly and just froze.
• That freeze response says so much — even with encouragement, the group setting was just too overwhelming for her. What would an ideal group therapy session look like for your child, given everything you've seen about how she responds?
 → I think an ideal group therapy session for my child would be a group where she could interact freely but not have to be forced to.
• That balance — being included without being put on the spot — sounds like it would make a real difference for her. When you picture that, is it more about the size of the group, the way the therapist runs it, or something else entirely?
 → I think a smaller size group, one to two people versus five to 10 would be better for her.
• How important are each of the following in a group therapy program for your child?
 → Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Affordable cost: Very important | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Very important | Consistent session structure: Somewhat important | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical
• I'd like to share an idea with you. Imagine a group therapy program for children where a licensed therapist leads every session, but is supported by an AI assistant. The AI helps manage the session — for example, making sure every child gets a turn to speak, guiding structured activities like storytelling or role-playing, and gently encouraging quieter children to participate. The therapist remains fully in control and can step in at any time. The AI simply helps the session run more smoothly and inclusively. → Continue
• What's your initial reaction to that idea?
 → I think that is a great idea. I do wish each child would have access to their own personal AI to be able to integrate fairness and ability to openly and freely speak evenly.
• That's an interesting instinct — almost like each child having their own private channel to engage through, so no one has to compete for space. What makes you feel that individual AI access would help specifically for a child like yours who tends to freeze up?

 → I think AI access would make me and my child both have options that prevent freezing up.
• It sounds like having a lower-stakes way to interact — where she doesn't have to speak up in front of the group — could be a real game-changer for her. Are there any reservations you have about the AI side of it, or does it feel mostly positive to you?

 → I think it is a good positive experience if AI could be able to speak for her and interact in ways that she was not able to speak for herself.
• How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?
 → Very comfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → I think each child should have access to their own ability to opt out or ability to use AI if they are comfortable and like what it is capable of.
• That idea of choice and control — letting the child decide how much to lean on the AI — feels really aligned with what you described about not wanting her to feel forced. What concerns, if any, do you have about AI being involved in your child's therapy?

 → I think AI would be positive as long as it was positive and controlled.
• When you say "controlled" — are you thinking about things like the AI staying on appropriate topics, or more about making sure it doesn't say something that could accidentally upset a child who's already emotionally sensitive?
 → I think it should be guided with a human. It shouldn't be fully AI. A regular person should be there to direct or be able to step in at all times.
• That human backstop sounds really important to you — the AI as a tool, not a replacement. What about privacy: does it concern you at all that an AI might be recording or processing what your child says during a session?

 → Sure, there are privacy concerns, but I am more concerned if AI takes the therapy the wrong direction.
• That concern about AI taking things in the wrong direction — especially with a child who's already emotionally vulnerable — makes a lot of sense. How would you feel about your child attending group therapy via video call rather than in person?

 → I'm unsure if she would enjoy video call. There would be anxiety about using the phone as well.
• The anxiety extending to the technology itself — not just the people in the session — is a really important layer. Do you think that's something she could warm up to over time, or does the screen itself feel like a barrier that would be hard to get past?

 → I think she could warm up to it over time, especially knowing that it's not a real person she's speaking to, but a guided computer.
• If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?
 → Definitely would try it
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?
 → I think this would be a very convenient option to be able to integrate her ability to speak through the AI or communicate with it in more than one way, especially outside of just talking. Having an AI she could text to or select options as to how she feels, or an AI that could communicate to the therapist for her what she has said

- Open to group therapy and telehealth; wants results tracked over time.

column10: It was about 6 months ago. It felt hurtful because no one told me anything despite my efforts to communicate with my son and his mother. We don’t get along, which creates issues with my son, and since losing split custody last August things feel weird.
column11: My son has ADHD and goes to therapy bi-weekly. He’s a good kid but shuts down, and our verbal relationship is okay but not great. Lately I’m frustrated because he’ll tell me one thing and someone else another; he lies and it’s upsetting.
column12: Communication has been the most frustrating part. My son’s mother never tells me anything and I find out much later. I’ve emailed asking for transparency when something is wrong and get no response, which feels very unprofessional. I don’t have an issue with therapy—I attend too—but I don’t trust my son’s current therapist.
column13: He attended group therapy at school; he’s off for summer and starting middle school in the fall. He likes a mix of group and 1:1. He was quiet at first and slow to warm up, but participated over the last two years. Correct meds, growing confidence, and interacting with kids helped.
column14: I don’t have an answer for what ideal looks like. I want him not to shut down—his abilities are amazing—and I want him to soar, not sink.
column15: Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Very important | Affordable cost: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Consistent session structure: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Critical

It’s upsetting, but the chance to interact with other kids and meet people seems helpful. I’m mixed because AI can be manipulated and can’t truly gauge him, though it might provide useful data.
column18: Results over time would make me comfortable. I want to see changes in how he acts, listens, and feels about himself, and watch for signs like becoming mean.
column19: I want data to make the decision. I worry both about safety/outcomes and the AI saying the wrong thing—my son is very literal so everything must align. He tends to believe everything from AI or YouTube is true.
column20: I’m open to either in-person or video. It has pros and cons; as long as he’s speaking to someone, it could work.
column22: Transparency from the provider. Consistent updates about what I need to know, especially safety-related: if he might harm himself or others, or any critical issues because he’s vulnerable and easily influenced.

• What's your initial reaction to that idea?
 → He didn't give me much of an answer, just said "lt's fine" and ignored me
• Sounds like you didn’t get much from him on it. For you personally, what’s your gut reaction to the idea?

 → I think it would help. These are critical years and if I don't support him in all the ways I can, what's the point? So yes, it would help!
• You really want to give him every chance to grow. What about it feels most helpful for a child like your son?

 → I'm not sure, its an upsetting thing.  The chance to interact with other kids I suppose and I don't know what, maybe meet people
• It makes sense that this brings up a lot. What, if anything, gives you pause or concern about AI being part of something this personal for him?

 → Because it can be manipulated and can't truly gauge him, however it can give data. So it's a mixed thing
• How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?
 → Somewhat comfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → Results. I am open to it but I want to see of anything changes. The answer is time
• You’d want proof over time. What kind of change would tell you it’s actually helping him?

 → How he acts, listens, feels about himself. If he's getting mean
• What concerns, if any, do you have about AI being involved in your child's therapy?

 → I just want data to make that decision.
• You’d want something concrete before trusting it. Are you mostly looking for data about safety and outcomes, or are you also concerned the AI could miss important cues or say the wrong thing?

 → A bit of both but the wrong thing. My son is very literal so everything would have to align.
• Since he’s very literal, what kind of wrong thing would worry you most an AI might say or do with him?

 → He thinks everything from AI or Youtube is true. Everything
• That makes a lot of sense. How would you feel about your child attending group therapy via video call rather than in person?

 → I'm open to either to be honest
• What would make video call work just as well for him as in person?

 → Because he's speaking to someone, there is a lot to consider and it has pros and cons
• If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?
 → Not sure
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?

 → Transparency from the provider
• When you say transparency, what would you want the provider to consistently share with you?

 → Just updates about what I need to know. He is vulnerable and easily influenced. I'm basically saying I want to know if he is going to kill himself, harm himself, or any other things I need to know.
