column4:
- I’m autistic and my child is autistic (formerly Asperger’s).
- I value self-worth building, school accommodations, and therapists who specialize in autism.
- Group therapy should be low-pressure, voluntary, and with autistic peers; start with fun, non-competitive activities.
- I’m comfortable with AI as a background tool under a present, engaged therapist; privacy and no data sharing are crucial.
- Prefer in-person for socialization but open to lower-cost virtual options for convenience and logistics.
column5: Autistic parent seeking safe, specialized support
column7: I have children between 6 and 12 years old
column8: Tutoring or academic support, Art or music classes, Therapy or counseling, Sports or physical activities
column9: Emotional regulation challenges, Difficulty making friends or socializing, Behavioral issues at school, Difficulty with focus or attention, Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem
column10: My child has autism (formerly Asperger’s), and we saw a counselor who specializes in autistic children to give him tools to navigate life. I wanted him to learn that he’s worthy and not broken, unlike how I felt growing up with high-functioning autism and being treated poorly by teachers. The visit felt positive: the counselor taught at his level, emphasized self-worth, and understood autism, including foundation skills and advocating for school accommodations. Based on his interest and the fit, I’m open to continuing long term as long as he’s comfortable.
column11:
Well, he was diagnosed with Asperger's high-functioning autism. I knew it, being somebody that has it myself. I just saw the struggles, especially there's a bully and they got in a confrontation. Now, I taught him to stand up for himself, and that he does. But he has low self-esteem in many areas and doesn't know how to express himself. He definitely has outbursts, things like that. 

I just don't want to repeat what happened to me because I felt like I was abused by teachers, honestly, verbally for being different. I'm not going to have that happen.
column12:
It's hard to find somebody who is very knowledgeable about autism. Therapy in general is just more related to mental health, and that's all well and good, but it's hard to get that to sink in for a child. Also, finding somebody that knows how to do children's therapy work or counseling is just very challenging because I feel like there's more adult-related things out there.

Finding somebody who specifically had a lot of knowledge in autism is challenging because sometimes you have to drive further away to get to them.
column13: He hasn’t done group therapy yet. I expect he’d be nervous and resistant at first; he articulates well one-on-one with an adult but is variable with peers. If he understood the group was other autistic kids with similar experiences (bullying, sensory overload, meltdowns) and not combative classmates, I think he could participate.
column14: An ideal group would be a safe, low-pressure space with only autistic children, where participation is always voluntary and no one is put on the spot. Starting with light, fun, child-friendly activities (e.g., creative art projects or non-competitive games) would help, followed by gentle discussion on navigating the world and sharing perspectives. The goal is building community and helping him see he’s not alone or abnormal, and maybe make friends.
column15: Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Critical | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Critical | Affordable cost: Critical | Consistent session structure: Very important | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical
column16: I’m positive about it if a licensed therapist leads and the AI stays in a background support role (productivity, turn-taking, structure). The therapist must be present and engaged; AI shouldn’t lead. I didn’t see anything negative at first glance.
column18:
Everything that's been described so far, I would feel comfortable as it's being led by a therapist. There has to be a therapist present. It's all fair. There is no favoritism amongst the group. So for me, I feel fully comfortable.

I would just, maybe they could show a sample of how this would go down for parents if we needed to see that. But for me, I just need to speak to the therapist. I would just want to speak to them privately and maybe have some private sessions with them to see how he was doing, things like that. You just need that human contact. There has to be a lot of elements of that.
column19:
Yes, I would prefer that specific, other than just a general name, but I would prefer that specific things like anything related to my child as a person—I guess we're talking, so we're not talking about social security numbers, but just any other personal information—I would not want that shared. And yes, they would need to prove that this is a completely private, no third party sharing, doesn't include anything invasive, just a name and absolutely no picture taking.

And misinterpreting emotional states—well, I still think it should be the therapist that does most of the talking. The AI can just present kind of like a general idea. And if anything like the chatbots that I have interacted with personally, they're rather, if I can say the word friendly, I've always found them to be friendly, non-combative. So just taking that idea and expanding it into more of a child-centric, not using complicated phrases and just getting it down to their level would be acceptable.
column20: I’m open to video sessions but would prefer in-person for socialization benefits; virtual should be significantly cheaper. In-person provides critical real-time social interaction with similar kids, which I value for autistic children. I wouldn’t rule out virtual, especially if logistics make in-person hard.
column22: Convenience would be the main reason—likely as an addition to current one-on-one therapy rather than a replacement. A virtual option could help overcome logistical barriers of coordinating families and make the program feasible to run at scale. I’d be interested to see it laid out.
column23: Parent of autistic child, Child aged 6-12
column24: I’m also autistic and had negative experiences with teachers, which motivates me to seek better support and advocacy for my child.
column30:
• Which of the following best describes your household? → I have children between 6 and 12 years old
• Which of these activities has your child participated in during the past 12 months? → Tutoring or academic support, Art or music classes, Therapy or counseling, Sports or physical activities
• Which of the following challenges has your child experienced? Select all that apply. → Emotional regulation challenges, Difficulty making friends or socializing, Behavioral issues at school, Difficulty with focus or attention, Shyness or social anxiety, Low self-confidence or self-esteem
• Tell me about the last time your child attended a therapy or counseling session. What was it like?
 → So, my child that went to therapy counseling, he has autism. And this was a specific counselor that deals with autism in children, formerly known as Asperger's is what he has. But just to give him tools to navigate life, because I really wish I had had that as someone growing up who also has high-functioning autism. 

And I just felt like it was needed to maybe give him tools to succeed, because there is a lot of challenges that are faced between children who have autism and who don't. And I thought it was a positive visit overall. I like the counselor.
• It sounds like you’re really proactive about giving him the tools you wish you’d had yourself. Since you mentioned it was a positive visit, what was the specific moment or interaction that made you feel like it was actually working for him?

 → Well, he does seem very curious to want to learn about how he can navigate the world. And we want to make sure that he understands there is nothing wrong with you, you are not broken. I felt like I was broken and that everything was wrong with me because that's how teachers treated me. 

But this counselor was making sure just to, in a child's way, at their level, teach them about you are worthy, you are just as good as everybody else, there is nothing wrong with you. They seem to actually understand autism because that's exactly what we were in there for, is all the foundation building skills and how it's different and how we can also advocate to the school for better accommodations. So those are things that are working. His interests showed that this has potential to be something I would want to continue for long term, as long as he'll let me without too much of a fuss.
• It’s wonderful that he’s responding so well to that focus on self-worth. Looking back to when you first started, what was the specific turning point or challenge that made you decide it was time to seek out that support?

 → Well, he was diagnosed with Asperger's high-functioning autism. I knew it, being somebody that has it myself. I just saw the struggles, especially there's a bully and they got in a confrontation. Now, I taught him to stand up for himself, and that he does. But he has low self-esteem in many areas and doesn't know how to express himself. He definitely has outbursts, things like that. 

I just don't want to repeat what happened to me because I felt like I was abused by teachers, honestly, verbally for being different. I'm not going to have that happen.
• It sounds like you are fiercely protective of him, especially given your own difficult experiences with teachers. What has been the most frustrating part of finding and maintaining the right therapy for your child?

 → It's hard to find somebody who is very knowledgeable about autism. Therapy in general is just more related to mental health, and that's all well and good, but it's hard to get that to sink in for a child. Also, finding somebody that knows how to do children's therapy work or counseling is just very challenging because I feel like there's more adult-related things out there.

Finding somebody who specifically had a lot of knowledge in autism is challenging because sometimes you have to drive further away to get to them.
• It sounds like the combination of finding someone who truly understands autism and the logistical burden of travel makes it a real uphill battle. Since you've had to navigate those hurdles, has your son ever participated in a group therapy setting, or is that something he hasn't experienced yet?

 → No, we haven't done a group therapy thing yet. Nothing like that. It's just mostly trying to see how he would even respond to sessions, letting him know that he is worthy and is just as good as everybody else, and trying to build the self-esteem, trying to teach him how to work with... we don't even like calling it a disability, work with his condition. They use much more proper words than I can really think of off the top of my head.

But anyway, how to work within the limits of autism. Because I mean, there are limits, but how to better approach things that work for autistic people instead of neurotypicals. But no, no group therapy. Sorry, I went off tangent.
• That’s not a tangent at all—it’s really helpful context. Since he hasn't been in a group setting, what do you imagine that experience might be like for him, and do you have any concerns about whether a quieter child like him would actually get enough attention or feel comfortable participating in a group?

 → Well, yes, I don't think he would take too well to it. I think he would be very nervous. He wouldn't wanna go. He can talk very, very well and articulate himself with just one adult, but when it comes to other children, it depends. 

If we could tell him, "Hey, these are kids that are also just like you and experience the same struggles with, you know, maybe they're a bully or just having some sensory overload meltdown," just things that he can relate to that he experiences. I think getting it through to him that these are not people like your classmates who are gonna be combative and attacking. So yeah, I think it's doable to actually get him to participate.
• It sounds like the biggest hurdle would be helping him see that a group of peers could be a safe space rather than another source of conflict. Given that, what would an ideal group therapy session look like for him—what kind of environment or activities would make him feel safe enough to open up?

 → Yes, safe space is exactly the right word. Ideal, it would definitely have to be with only other autistic children, and maybe they are also sharing some of their struggles, and maybe he can meet some friends out of it. It would just need to be very low pressure, nothing where anybody was put on the spot. Low pressure is really the word that I'm looking for.

Everything should be voluntary. If you want to share, you share. Not where they go around the group and say, "All right, you're gonna share whether you like it or not." I don't like that approach. I think everything should be voluntary, and then that might help him to open up if some of the others are more willing, or maybe they give prompts or things.

It also helps you to see that you're not alone. I felt like I was alone, like there was nobody like me. So I think that's beneficial for him to see that he's not abnormal or alone. There's plenty of people like this.
• It sounds like creating that sense of community and removing the pressure to perform is really key for him. Since you mentioned how important it is for him to feel like he's not alone, what specific activities or topics do you think would help him connect with those other kids without feeling put on the spot?

 → So maybe my ideal is turning into more of an autism get-together support group instead of a therapy. But if I'm creating my ideal for specific activities, just start with some fun activities. It doesn't have to be anything super serious where we go around and talk about our struggles. Start with some fun activities that are lighthearted, things that would appeal to children. Everybody can talk about things that interest them. 

I would think it needs to start off positive, and then they can delve into more of the heavier topics about how to navigate the world and then hearing other children's perspective. I just emphasize the part about not feeling like you're alone because it affected me so badly. So I would just say, whether it's a creative art project where everybody sits down and does it or a little fun game where nobody has to have any rankings. I think anything that makes everybody feel equal, a game or a project, stuff like that.
• How important are each of the following in a group therapy program for your child?
 → Consistent session structure: Very important | Therapist gives individual attention: Critical | Every child gets equal opportunity to participate: Critical | Sessions are engaging and fun for the child: Critical | Progress is tracked and shared with parents: Critical | Affordable cost: Critical
• I'd like to share an idea with you. Imagine a group therapy program for children where a licensed therapist leads every session, but is supported by an AI assistant. The AI helps manage the session — for example, making sure every child gets a turn to speak, guiding structured activities like storytelling or role-playing, and gently encouraging quieter children to participate. The therapist remains fully in control and can step in at any time. The AI simply helps the session run more smoothly and inclusively. → Continue
• What's your initial reaction to that idea?
 → Oh yeah. I mean, I don't see why using the AI would be a negative thing in this context because there is a licensed therapist there making sure everything is still run properly because this is obviously something that needs human attention. But if it's just used as a tool and more in the background for productivity tracking and some of the other things that it said, that would not bother me. I wouldn't see that as bad.

You just have to have the therapist present and they actually have to be engaging. It can't just be all led by AI obviously because these are kids. So you need the structure of the therapist. But yeah, right off the bat, I didn't see anything negative.
• That makes sense—you see the AI as a helpful tool for structure, provided the human therapist is still the one truly connecting with the kids. What specifically about the AI's role in managing the session, like ensuring everyone gets a turn, feels most helpful to you?

 → Well, I guess the AI would handle more of the background tasks. Maybe it might be kind of fun if the AI was also present in the room and was to change to a different task and kind of have an interactive thing where maybe all the kids get to say what task they want to do, and everybody gets to do a task. But the AI just kind of is in the background moderating, making sure everybody does it.

So ensuring everybody gets a turn, I mean, there's always human error. I don't think a therapist would ever make any error, but maybe it could pop up on the screen that, "Hey, this is so-and-so's turn." That way it's a visual for the children too, and not just the therapist saying, "Okay, so it's so-and-so's turn." So it pops up on like a screen. I mean, I'm just trying to understand how this would work. I imagine there would be a screen there, so it would pop up on the screen, I would think.
• That visual element of having the AI prompt turns on a screen is a really interesting way to take the pressure off the therapist and the kids. Since you've thought about how that might look in practice, what concerns, if any, do you have about the AI potentially missing a social cue or saying something that might not land right with a child who is sensitive to how they're being spoken to?

 → Well, getting the AI to learn how to speak to autistic children in a way that is appropriate—now, I'm not really sure it should be doing any of the speaking. I think it should just be doing more of the background tasks. So the therapist would still be the one doing all the work. It would just maybe, like I said, show up on the screen that this is so-and-so's turn.

For dealing with sensitive children, how they need to be spoken to, that's still the therapist's job. That's what concern I would have is that it does—I don't really know if it's good for an AI to try to be talking to kids like this because unless there was one that was solely developed for autistic people, I'd be open-minded to see the possibilities of this.
• How comfortable would you be with your child participating in a session like this?
 → Very comfortable
• What would need to be true for you to feel fully comfortable with this kind of program?

 → Everything that's been described so far, I would feel comfortable as it's being led by a therapist. There has to be a therapist present. It's all fair. There is no favoritism amongst the group. So for me, I feel fully comfortable.

I would just, maybe they could show a sample of how this would go down for parents if we needed to see that. But for me, I just need to speak to the therapist. I would just want to speak to them privately and maybe have some private sessions with them to see how he was doing, things like that. You just need that human contact. There has to be a lot of elements of that.
• It sounds like having that direct, human connection with the therapist is the anchor that makes the rest of the technology feel safe and acceptable. Beyond the need for that human oversight, what specific concerns do you have about the AI itself—for instance, are you worried about how it handles your child's private data, or perhaps that it might misinterpret his emotional state during a session?

 → Yes, I would prefer that specific, other than just a general name, but I would prefer that specific things like anything related to my child as a person—I guess we're talking, so we're not talking about social security numbers, but just any other personal information—I would not want that shared. And yes, they would need to prove that this is a completely private, no third party sharing, doesn't include anything invasive, just a name and absolutely no picture taking.

And misinterpreting emotional states—well, I still think it should be the therapist that does most of the talking. The AI can just present kind of like a general idea. And if anything like the chatbots that I have interacted with personally, they're rather, if I can say the word friendly, I've always found them to be friendly, non-combative. So just taking that idea and expanding it into more of a child-centric, not using complicated phrases and just getting it down to their level would be acceptable.
• That makes total sense—you're prioritizing his privacy and ensuring the AI stays in a supportive, non-invasive role. Since you mentioned the importance of that human connection, how would you feel about your child attending these group sessions via video call rather than in person?

 → I'd be open to it. I think the price would definitely have to be significantly lower because I think that it's still important for the socialization aspect of people that have autism. It's important to have early socialization and positive experiences. So I would still prefer a physical setting, but I would not totally rule out the virtual setting either.
• It sounds like you see the value in the convenience of telehealth, but you're weighing that against the irreplaceable benefit of in-person social practice. Given that, what is the biggest thing you feel he would miss out on if the sessions were strictly virtual?

 → Definitely missed out on the needed socialization because I think that the visual right in front of you of children that are just like you and being able to actually speak and interact with them and having that social setting is very important. Especially, like I said, for autistic people, it's something that, heck, it's something I wish I was introduced to at a young age. So I just think that the social components would be very lacking if it was only virtual.
• If a program like this existed today — clinician-led group therapy with AI support, delivered via telehealth, for $50-100 per session — how likely would you be to try it?
 → Probably would try it
• What would make you choose this over your child's current therapy arrangement?

 → Well, I would definitely be very open to the in-person group, but convenience would be a big factor of it. It'd be very convenient. So maybe it could be something that's done along with the current one-on-one arrangement. I'd say convenience would be the biggest factor of me choosing that.
• It sounds like the convenience of a hybrid approach—keeping his one-on-one sessions while adding this group experience—could be a really powerful way to support his growth. Is there anything else you’d want to share about your hopes for his journey or what you’d look for in a program like this?

 → No, not really. I would just be interested if there was a program like this because it does seem like there is limitations in programs. So maybe if it was virtual, that could actually make it happen and expand it.

I think when things are in person, trying to get a bunch of people together, like the parents and the kids, that can make it logistically difficult. So maybe the virtual would make things smoother and easier, and it could actually be successfully pulled off. So I'd love to see if this could be laid out.
